findings.description: The yellow/highlighted row styling from the PDF table is not preserved in the HTML table
findings.evidence: PDF shows alternating yellow-highlighted rows in the table; HTML renders rows with plain white/grey alternating style

findings.description: Table header formatting differs slightly - PDF uses a compact multi-line header with # symbol on separate line, HTML uses single-line headers
findings.evidence: PDF header shows '#' on one line and 'COMP' on next line; HTML shows '# COMP CASES' as a single header

findings.description: The HTML table rows have significantly more vertical spacing than the PDF, making the table much taller
findings.evidence: PDF fits rows 1-37 compactly on one page; HTML spreads the same rows across much more vertical space requiring more scrolling

findings.description: The yellow/highlighted row styling from the PDF table is not preserved in the HTML table on page 2
findings.evidence: PDF shows alternating yellow-highlighted rows; HTML renders plain styling

findings.description: The yellow/highlighted row styling from the PDF table is not preserved in the HTML table on page 3
findings.evidence: PDF shows alternating yellow-highlighted rows; HTML renders plain styling

findings.description: The STATE total row in the PDF is bold and visually distinct; the HTML preserves the bold formatting
findings.evidence: Both PDF and HTML show STATE row in bold - this is correctly preserved

findings.description: STATE AGENCY row in PDF has no accuracy rate value but shows 22.14% error rate; HTML matches this correctly
findings.evidence: Both versions show STATE AGENCY with 91 comp cases, 91 invalid cases, blank accuracy rate, and 22.14% error rate

findings.description: Nested <main> elements in HTML. The document has a <main> inside another <main>, which is invalid HTML structure, though it does not affect content fidelity.
findings.evidence: HTML has <main> wrapping the page-header div, then another <main> wrapping the table.
